attributes.stem:
A 30-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with acute onset left-sided abdominal pain following intercourse. The pain is associated with nausea. She has no family history of ovarian or breast cancer and takes the progesterone only pill for contraception.

On examination, her pulse rate is 70/min, blood pressure 120/70mmHg, temperature 36.5oC and respiratory rate 18/min. There is tenderness in the left iliac fossa but no guarding or rigidity. The speculum examination is unremarkable and a bimanual examination reveals left adnexal tenderness. The urinary beta HCG is negative and complete blood picture and biochemistry are normal. A transvaginal ultrasound is performed which demonstrates no ovarian torsion, but several small homogenous, anechoic, unilocular ovarian cysts bilaterally. The largest cyst is 25 mm in diameter. A small amount of peritoneal free fluid is present.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management?
attributes.explanation:
This woman’s history and ultrasound findings are typical of a simple ovarian cyst rupture. Ovarian malignancy is also less common in premenopausal women (excluding those with an oncogenic mutation such as BRCA-1 or BRCA-2) and the ultrasound report of small, homogenous, and unilocular masses is reassuring. A simple cyst rupture in a patient who is haemodynamically stable and with minimal amounts of intraperitoneal fluid can be managed on an outpatient basis with simple analgesia and reassurance that the pain typically resolves within 72 hours.

Per RCOG guidelines, asymptomatic simple cysts 30-50 mm do not require follow-up, cysts 50-70 mm require ultrasound follow-up, and cysts more than 70 mm should be considered for either further imaging (MRI) or surgical intervention. However, as this patient is symptomatic, follow-up imaging via ultrasound in 6-8 weeks is warranted regardless of cyst size.

Methotrexate is used in a range of malignancies, and in medical management for ectopic pregnancy. Its use is not indicated in this patient.

A dilatation and curettage allows assessment of the endometrium and is not useful in the management of ovarian cysts.

A laparoscopic cystectomy may be considered if the cysts are greater than 5cm, causing significant ongoing symptoms, or are shown to be complex (e.g. dermoid cyst). Given that this woman’s cysts appear to be simple in nature and her symptoms can be expected to resolve spontaneously, cystectomy is not indicated.

A bilateral oophorectomy may be considered in ovarian malignancy or postmenopausal women with an adnexal mass. It is not the appropriate management of ovarian cysts in reproductive aged women.
learningPoints: Most cases of simple ovarian cyst rupture in young woman can be managed conservatively.
references: Yeoh M. Investigation and management of an ovarian mass. Aust Fam Physician. 2015 Jan-Feb;44(1-2):48-52. PMID: 25688960. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25688960/

Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists. Management of suspected ovarian masses in premenopausal women (Green-top Guideline No. 62) [Internet]. London: RCOG; 2011 [cited 2024 Nov 23]. Available from: https://www.rcog.org.uk/guidance/browse-all-guidance/green-top-guidelines/ovarian-masses-in-preme...
Bethune M. How to manage a complex ovarian cyst. O&G Magazine. 2020 Autumn;22(1). Available from: https://www.ogmagazine.org.au/22/1-22/how-to-manage-a-complex-ovarian-cyst/
